evals.prompt: Ops keeps seeing production mistakes when admins delete environments. I want to stop this by adding an Undo button for all destructive actions, maybe snapshots every 15 minutes plus a restore endpoint. We want it because people keep pressing delete by mistake. Can you help me move this forward quickly?
evals.expected_output: The brainstorming output should reframe the request around irreversible-damage reduction and destructive-action recovery rather than locking onto snapshots and restore endpoints, while surfacing the missing reversibility, ownership, and readiness semantics.
expectations: Separates the desired outcome from the proposed implementation idea of snapshots, undo, or restore endpoint
Defines the actor, failure mode, and desired behavior delta for destructive admin actions
Surfaces hidden invariants or acceptance semantics such as reversibility limits, restore idempotency, authorization boundaries, tenant blast radius, or external dependency scope
Lists material constraints that would shape design, such as retention window, rollback objective, audit visibility, tenant isolation, or third-party resource ownership
Includes blocking questions with owner and unblock condition rather than generic follow-ups
Makes an explicit readiness decision grounded in the unresolved rollback and ownership semantics

evals.prompt: We need "Cancel now" for paid subscriptions. Current flow cancels at period end. Product says just cancel plus disable tokens now and run refund in background; engineering says use async webhooks and retries. We need this by end of month with less support load. No architecture changes yet. Please turn this into a solid design-ready problem frame.
evals.expected_output: The brainstorming output should separate the user promise from implementation ideas, make the time-zero semantics of cancellation explicit, and expose billing and idempotency blockers before deeper design.
expectations: Separates the requested business outcome from the provisional implementation ideas such as webhooks, retries, or background refund processing
Defines the behavior delta and acceptance semantics for `cancel now`, including immediate access or entitlement behavior and refund expectations
Flags hidden invariants or race conditions such as repeated cancel requests, webhook replay, double refunds, or status divergence across services
Lists material constraints such as provider event behavior, refund policy or compliance, time guarantees, or support-load objective
Includes blocking questions with owner and unblock condition for billing, legal, or product semantics
Makes an explicit readiness decision based on whether cancel semantics and refund policy are sufficiently defined

evals.prompt: Legal approved policy says users can request account deletion any time; personal data must be removed from user-facing systems within 7 days. Security policy says we must keep audit evidence for 7 years. Compliance says we can keep pseudonymous identifiers only in logs. We are adding account deletion in user-service only, no UI redesign, no data warehouse changes. Help us frame this request before design, and tell us if it’s ready.
evals.expected_output: The brainstorming output should frame the request as a deletion-vs-retention obligation problem, define the real scope boundary, and make data-class and policy blockers explicit without drifting into implementation.
expectations: Frames the problem as concurrent obligations around user-visible erasure and long-term evidentiary retention rather than as a local delete implementation task
Separates policy interpretation and problem framing from implementation design choices
Surfaces hidden invariants or acceptance semantics such as no plaintext PII in retained artifacts, data-class boundaries, pseudonymization scope, or cross-service deletion boundary
Flags the scope gap created by `user-service only` and names cross-service or policy implications instead of treating the boundary as harmless
Includes blocking questions with owner and unblock condition, including at least one legal, security, or compliance dependency
Makes an explicit readiness decision with rationale grounded in the remaining policy and scope ambiguity
